title: Binary Search
statement: Given an array of integers `nums` which is sorted in ascending order, and an integer `target`, write a function to search `target` in `nums`. If `target` exists, then return its index. Otherwise, return `-1`.

bruteForce.description: Scan the array sequentially from start to end to find the target. (Linear Search).

optimized.description: Use two pointers, `left` (0) and `right` (N-1), representing the active search window. While `left <= right`, calculate the midpoint `mid`. If `nums[mid] == target`, return `mid`. If `nums[mid] < target`, shrink window to the right: `left = mid + 1`. Otherwise, shrink to the left: `right = mid - 1`.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Initialize boundary pointers covering the entire array.
Calculate midpoint. Using l + (r - l) // 2 prevents integer overflow compared to (l + r) // 2.
If target is found, return its index immediately.
If target is larger, ignore the left half by moving the left pointer past mid.
If target is smaller, ignore the right half by moving the right pointer before mid.

cppExplanation.explanation: Compute midpoint safely without integer overflow risk.
Return index if target value matches midpoint element.

timeComplexity: O(log N) - The search space is divided in half at each step.
spaceComplexity: O(1) - Only constant extra variables are used.
dryRun: Step 2: l = 3, r = 5. mid = 3 + (5-3)//2 = 4. nums[4] = 9 == 9. Target found! Return 4.
followUps: Can you write it recursively? (Yes, passes boundaries as parameters, stack depth O(log N))
commonMistakes: Using `(l + r) // 2` which can cause integer overflow in languages like C++ when left and right are very large.
whenNotToUse: If the array is unsorted, binary search cannot be used unless we sort it first (which takes O(N log N) time).

title: Search Insert Position
statement: Given a sorted array of distinct integers and a target value, return the index if the target is found. If not, return the index where it would be if it were inserted in order.

bruteForce.description: Scan sequentially to find first element >= target. (Linear search).

optimized.description: Run standard binary search. If target is found, return its index. If loop terminates without finding target, the `left` pointer will end up pointing to the first element larger than target, which is the correct insertion index.

pythonExplanation.explanation: If target is found, return its index.
If loop finishes, left pointer represents the correct insertion index because it points to the first value >= target.

cppExplanation.explanation: When search bounds collapse, the left pointer index is the exact insert location.

timeComplexity: O(log N) - Standard binary search halving of search space.
dryRun: l = 2, r = 3. mid = 2 (val=5) == 5 -> Target found at index 2.
edgeCases: Target belongs at end index N
followUps: Can you write this using libraries? (In Python, `bisect.bisect_left(nums, target)`. In C++, `lower_bound`)
commonMistakes: Returning `mid` or `l - 1` on mismatch instead of `l`.

title: First Bad Version
statement: You are a product manager and currently leading a team to develop a new product. Unfortunately, the latest version of your product fails the quality check. Since each version is developed based on the previous version, all the versions after a bad version are also bad. Find the first bad version.

bruteForce.description: Call the API `isBadVersion(i)` sequentially for all versions from 1 to n. (Slow).

optimized.description: Use binary search. Let `left = 1` and `right = n`. While `left < right`, calculate `mid`. If `isBadVersion(mid)` is true, the first bad version must be at or before `mid` (set `right = mid`). Otherwise, it must be after `mid` (set `left = mid + 1`). When the loop terminates, `left` will point to the first bad version.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Loop until pointers meet. Do not use l <= r since right is set to mid (would cause infinite loop).
If mid is bad, it is a candidate. Move right boundary to mid to search earlier versions.
If mid is good, all versions up to mid are good. First bad must be to the right.

cppExplanation.explanation: Search loop ends when l == r, indicating the first bad version.
Include mid in the search space since it could be the first bad version.

timeComplexity: O(log N) - Halves search space of size N at each iteration.
dryRun: l = 1, r = 5. mid = 3. isBad(3) = False -> l = 4.
l = 4, r = 5. mid = 4. isBad(4) = True -> r = 4.
l = 4, r = 4. Loop terminates. First bad version = 4.
followUps: Can you explain why we don't use `l <= r`? (Yes, because when `l == r`, `mid = l`. If `isBad(mid)` is true, setting `r = mid` would keep `l` and `r` identical, causing an infinite loop)
visualization:
Versions: 1   2   3   4   5
Status:   G   G   G   B   B
  L=1, R=5 -> mid=3 (Good) -> L=4
  L=4, R=5 -> mid=4 (Bad)  -> R=4
  L=4, R=4 -> Loop ends -> First bad = 4
commonMistakes: Using `r = mid - 1` when `isBadVersion(mid)` is true, which can skip the target if `mid` itself was the first bad version.

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
statement: Given an integer array `nums` sorted in ascending order (with distinct values) which is possibly rotated, and a target, return the index of target if it is in `nums`, else `-1`.

optimized.description: At least one half of a rotated sorted array is always normally sorted. Use binary search. Identify which half of `nums` is sorted: 1. If `nums[left] <= nums[mid]`, left half is sorted. Check if target lies in this sorted range; if so, search left (`right = mid - 1`), else search right. 2. Otherwise, right half is sorted. Check if target lies in this sorted range; if so, search right, else left.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Compare boundary to verify if the left half is normally sorted.
Check if the target falls within the sorted left half's bounds.
If left half is sorted but target is not in it, search the right half.

cppExplanation.explanation: Determine which half of the active range is continuously sorted.
If right half is sorted, check if target fits in its range.

timeComplexity: O(log N) - Standard binary search split step.
dryRun: Check sorted half: nums[0](4) <= nums[3](7) -> Left half is sorted. Is target 0 in [4, 7)? No. Search right -> l = 4.
Check sorted half: nums[4](0) <= nums[5](1) -> Left sorted. Is target 0 in [0, 1)? Yes! Search left -> r = 4.
l = 4, r = 4. mid = 4 (val=0) == 0 -> Found at index 4!
followUps: Can you solve it if array contains duplicates? (Search in Rotated Sorted Array II, worst case becomes O(N) when bounds match mid)
relatedProblems: Search in Rotated Sorted Array II
Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
visualization:
nums = [4, 5, 6, 7, 0, 1, 2]  Target = 0
  L=0       mid=3      R=6   -> Left [4..7] sorted. 0 not in left -> L=4
            L=4 mid=5  R=6   -> Right [1..2] sorted. 0 not in right -> R=4
            L=4 mid=4  R=4   -> 0 == 0 -> Found!
commonMistakes: Assuming the array is sorted normally and running basic binary search, which fails on rotation boundaries.

title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
statement: Suppose an array of length `n` sorted in ascending order is rotated between 1 and `n` times. Given the sorted rotated array `nums` of unique elements, return the minimum element of this array. Solve in O(log N) time.

bruteForce.description: Scan sequentially to find the smallest element. (Linear scan).

optimized.description: Use binary search. Let `left = 0` and `right = N-1`. While `left < right`, calculate `mid`. If `nums[mid] > nums[right]`, the pivot (minimum) must be to the right of `mid` (set `left = mid + 1`). Otherwise, the minimum is at or to the left of `mid` (set `right = mid`). When loop ends, `nums[left]` is the minimum.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Compare mid to the right boundary. If mid value is larger than right value, the rotation point (minimum) is in the right half.
Skip mid since it is larger than right, so it cannot be the minimum.
If mid is smaller than right, mid could be the minimum, or minimum is earlier.

cppExplanation.explanation: If mid element exceeds right element, rotation pivot resides in right portion.
Assign right to mid to narrow search to left-hand side including mid.

timeComplexity: O(log N) - Halves search space of size N at each iteration.
dryRun: l = 3, r = 3. Loop terminates. Min element is nums[3] = 1.
edgeCases: Array is not rotated (already sorted, returns nums[0])
followUps: Can you solve it if array contains duplicates? (Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array II, worst case degrades to O(N))
relatedProblems: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array II
visualization:
nums = [3, 4, 5, 1, 2]
  L=0       mid=2      R=4   -> 5 > 2 -> Minimum is in right half -> L=3
            L=3 mid=3  R=4   -> 1 <= 2 -> Minimum could be mid -> R=3
            L=3, R=3 -> Loop ends -> min is nums[3] = 1
commonMistakes: Comparing `nums[mid]` with `nums[l]` instead of `nums[r]`. Left comparison fails on unrotated arrays.

title: Search a 2D Matrix
statement: Write an efficient algorithm that searches for a value `target` in an `m x n` integer matrix. This matrix has properties: 1. Integers in each row are sorted from left to right. 2. The first integer of each row is greater than the last integer of the previous row.

bruteForce.description: Iterate through every row and run binary search on each row sequentially.

optimized.description: Treat the 2D matrix as a sorted 1D array of size `M * N`. Run standard binary search with `left = 0` and `right = M * N - 1`. Map the midpoint index `mid` to 2D coordinates using `row = mid // cols` and `col = mid % cols` to access the value.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Get the dimensions of the matrix.
Map the virtual 1D index mid to its corresponding 2D matrix cell index.
If value is smaller, search right in the virtual array.

cppExplanation.explanation: Convert virtual 1D index mid to 2D coordinates: row = mid / cols, col = mid % cols.

timeComplexity: O(log(M * N)) - Standard binary search on M * N elements.
dryRun: Step 1: mid = 4. val = matrix[4//3][4%3] = matrix[1][1] = 11 > 10. Set r = 3.
Step 2: l = 0, r = 3. mid = 1. val = matrix[1//3][1%3] = matrix[0][1] = 3 < 10. Set l = 2.
Step 3: l = 2, r = 3. mid = 2. val = matrix[2//3][2%3] = matrix[0][2] = 5 < 10. Set l = 3.
Step 4: l = 3, r = 3. mid = 3. val = matrix[3//3][3%3] = matrix[1][0] = 10 == 10. Found! Return True.
edgeCases: Target is smaller than first element
followUps: Can you solve it if the rows are sorted but the first element of a row is not larger than last of previous? (Search a 2D Matrix II, solved in O(M + N) by starting from top-right corner)
visualization:
Matrix:                     Virtual 1D Array:
[ [ 1,  3,  5 ],            [ 1, 3, 5, 10, 11, 16, 20, 23, 30 ]
  [10, 11, 16 ],             Index map: row = index / cols
  [20, 23, 30 ] ]                       col = index % cols
  Binary Search directly on virtual 1D array.
commonMistakes: Dividing mid by rows instead of cols (which maps coordinates incorrectly).

title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
statement: Given two sorted arrays `nums1` and `nums2` of size `m` and `n` respectively, return the median of the two sorted arrays. The overall run time complexity should be O(log (m+n)).

bruteForce.description: Merge both arrays in a sorted array in O(M+N) time, and return the middle element.

optimized.description: Ensure binary search runs on the smaller array to optimize time. Let `A` be the smaller array. Partition `A` and `B` into two halves such that the left half has size `(M+N+1)//2`. Binary search for partition index in `A`. Let `leftA` and `rightA` be the boundary values around partition in `A`. If `leftA <= rightB` and `leftB <= rightA`, the partition is correct. Else, adjust partition bounds.

pythonExplanation.line: if len(A) > len(B): A, B = B, A
pythonExplanation.explanation: Ensure binary search is done on the smaller array to run in O(log(min(M, N))) time.

pythonExplanation.line: leftA = A[i - 1] if i > 0 else float('-inf')
pythonExplanation.explanation: Assign negative infinity if partition index splits before the array starts.

pythonExplanation.line: if leftA <= rightB and leftB <= rightA:
pythonExplanation.explanation: Verify partition validity: all elements on left must be smaller than or equal to elements on right.

cppExplanation.explanation: Force binary search on the shorter vector to ensure optimal time complexity.
Assign INT_MIN if boundary index falls to the left of the array bounds.

timeComplexity: O(log(min(M, N))) - Binary search is executed on the smaller array A of size M.
dryRun: Sorted: A = [2], B = [1, 3] (swap since size of A is smaller). total=3, half=2.
Check: leftB(3) <= rightA(2)? False. Too small in A -> l = i + 1 = 1.
Check: leftA(2) <= rightB(3) and leftB(1) <= rightA(inf)? True! Partition correct.
Odd total -> max(leftA, leftB) = max(2, 1) = 2. Return 2.0.
edgeCases: Even sum of sizes vs. odd sum of sizes
followUps: Can you explain why division by 2.0 is needed for even total? (Yes, because median is the average of the two middle elements, which can be floating point numbers)
relatedProblems: Median of Two Sorted Arrays II
Kth Element of Two Sorted Arrays
visualization:
A:  [ 1, 3 ]  --> Partition i
B:  [ 2 ]     --> Partition j
Ensure A_left <= B_right and B_left <= A_right
Odd total: return max(A_left, B_left)
commonMistakes: Not ensuring that array A is the smaller array, leading to out-of-bounds index errors on array B partition index `j = half - i`.

title: Split Array Largest Sum
statement: Given an integer array `nums` and an integer `k`, split the array into `k` non-empty contiguous subarrays such that the largest sum of these subarrays is minimized. Return the minimized largest sum.

bruteForce.description: Exhaustively check all combinations of subarray partitions using backtracking. (O(N^K) time).

optimized.description: Use binary search on the answer range. The minimum possible largest sum is `max(nums)` (when each element is a subarray). The maximum possible sum is `sum(nums)` (when the entire array is one subarray). Set `left = max(nums)` and `right = sum(nums)`. For each `mid`, check if we can partition the array into <= `k` subarrays with maximum sum <= `mid`. If yes, search smaller sums (`right = mid`). Else, search larger sums (`left = mid + 1`).

pythonExplanation.explanation: Set the binary search range bounds: minimum possible sum is the maximum single element; maximum is the array total sum.
If adding the next element exceeds the capacity limit `max_sum`, start a new subarray.
If the number of required subarrays exceeds k, this capacity limit is invalid.
If the capacity is feasible, record it and try to find a smaller maximum sum.

cppExplanation.explanation: Use long long variables to avoid summation overflows during range computations.
If a partition with target maximum sum is achievable, narrow the binary search window to check for smaller maximums.

timeComplexity: O(N log(Sum - Max)) - Binary search runs log(Sum - Max) times, and the feasibility checker `can_partition` takes O(N) time per iteration.
dryRun: Step 1: mid = 21. Check partition limit 21: [7,2,5] (sum=14) and [10,8] (sum=18) -> 2 subarrays <= k(2). Feasible! res=21, r=20.
Step 2: mid = 15. Check partition limit 15: [7,2,5] (sum=14), [10] (sum=10), [8] (sum=8) -> 3 subarrays > k(2). Infeasible! l=16.
edgeCases: k matches size of array (returns max(nums))
followUps: Can you explain how this is related to Book Allocation Problem? (Yes, the Book Allocation Problem is identical: partitioning books pages to K students while minimizing the maximum pages read by any student)
relatedProblems: Capacity To Ship Packages Within D Days
commonMistakes: Setting the initial lower bound `l` to 0 or 1 instead of `max(nums)`, which causes invalid check loops because individual elements cannot fit in the capacity limit.

title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array II
statement: Given the sorted rotated array `nums` that may contain duplicates, return the minimum element of this array. Solve it in average O(log N) time.

bruteForce.description: Scan sequentially to find the minimum. (Linear scan).

optimized.description: Use binary search. Let `left = 0` and `right = N-1`. While `left < right`, calculate `mid`. If `nums[mid] > nums[right]`, the minimum is in the right half (`left = mid + 1`). If `nums[mid] < nums[right]`, the minimum is in the left half including `mid` (`right = mid`). If they are equal (`nums[mid] == nums[right]`), we cannot determine the half due to duplicates; decrement `right` by 1 to shrink the window safely.
optimized.timeComplexity: O(log N) average, O(N) worst case

pythonExplanation.explanation: If mid value is smaller than right value, the right half is sorted and minimum is in left half including mid.
If nums[mid] == nums[r], we cannot tell which side is sorted. Shrink the search window by 1 from the right safely.

cppExplanation.explanation: Duplicate collapse step: reduce search space from the right boundary by 1 when values match.

timeComplexity: O(log N) average - Duplicates are eliminated in O(1) steps on average. Worst case is O(N) when all elements are identical.
dryRun: l = 0, r = 5. mid = 2 (val=2) == nums[5](2) -> Cannot determine. Decrement: r = 4.
l = 3, r = 3. Loop terminates. Min element is nums[3] = 0.
edgeCases: All elements are identical (returns nums[0] after N decrements)
followUps: Can you explain why we decrement `right` instead of incrementing `left`? (Yes, because if we increment `left`, we might skip the minimum element if `nums[left]` was the minimum; decrementing `right` is safe because `nums[mid] == nums[right]` means the element at `right` is duplicated at `mid`, so it is not lost)
relatedProblems: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
Search in Rotated Sorted Array II
visualization:
nums = [2,  2,  2,  0,  1,  2]
  L=0       mid=2      R=5   -> 2 == 2 -> Cannot determine -> R=4
  L=0  mid=2  R=4            -> 2 > 1 -> Min is in right half -> L=3
  L=3, R=4 -> mid=3(0) <= 1  -> R=3 -> min is nums[3] = 0
commonMistakes: Assuming O(log N) worst case. Explain to students that worst case is O(N) due to duplicate values.
